metadata.title: Guideline on the evaluation of drugs for the treatment of Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease
metadata.note: Adopted by CHMP 17 March 2011, in effect 1 October 2011. Covers clinical development of new medicinal products for GORD in adults and children. Does not cover OTC products, generic drug development, or functional GI disorders.

rules.rule_text: Reflux oesophagitis must be diagnosed by endoscopy using the Los Angeles (L.A.) classification; other classification systems are no longer recommended but may be justified case by case.
rules.verbatim_quote: Reflux oesophagitis has to be diagnosed by endoscopy, using the best validated classification, which is, at the moment, the Los Angeles classification. [...] The use of other classifications is no longer recommended, but may be justified on a case by case basis.
rules.exceptions: Other classifications may be justified on a case-by-case basis.

rules.rule_text: A global indication of 'GORD' covering both erosive and non-erosive populations requires both populations to be studied in pivotal trials; however, the two populations must be tested in separate trials.
rules.verbatim_quote: A global indication 'GORD' may be possible if the two distinct populations are both studied in pivotal trials. However, the two populations should be tested in separate trials (see 6.3.3.1.).
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: For clinical trials in non-erosive GORD, endoscopic data confirming the exclusion of mucosal breaks are required before patient inclusion.
rules.verbatim_quote: If studies in this patient population are conducted, data on endoscopic diagnosis (exclusion of mucosal breaks) are required before inclusion (see 6.1.1.).
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: For trials in PPI partial or non-responders, insufficient PPI response must be documented during a run-in period of at least 4 weeks for non-erosive patients and at least 8 weeks for erosive disease patients; PPI compliance must be carefully documented.
rules.verbatim_quote: The insufficient response should always be documented during a run-in period with a duration of at least 4 weeks in patients with non-erosive, and 8 weeks in those with erosive disease. This period should emphasise and carefully document the compliance with PPI therapy as non-compliance is an important cause of PPI failure.
rules.exceptions: Not applicable when an add-on indication to acid suppressives is investigated.

rules.rule_text: Indication claims for 'atypical GORD' as a standalone indication (non-cardiac chest pain, chronic cough, laryngitis, asthma) cannot currently be recommended for regulatory purposes; companies should seek Scientific Advice.
rules.verbatim_quote: Therefore, the conduct of trials for regulatory purposes with the goal to claim an indication other than GORD, by defining a sub-population based on the nature of such 'atypical' symptoms, can currently not be recommended. For such development programs, companies should seek Scientific Advice in order to receive individual feedback/guidance.
rules.exceptions: Scientific Advice may provide individual guidance.

rules.rule_text: A treatment claim for functional heartburn (FH) is currently not considered acceptable as an indication for an investigational product due to insufficient validation of the FH concept.
rules.verbatim_quote: Any claim for the treatment of FH for an investigational product is however, currently not considered acceptable because of insufficient validation of this concept at the present time.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: Patients enrolled in GORD clinical trials based on symptoms must have both cardinal symptoms present (heartburn AND acid regurgitation), regardless of whether the primary endpoint is endoscopic or symptomatic.
rules.verbatim_quote: As the cardinal symptoms of GORD are regarded to be heartburn and acid regurgitation, the presence of both symptoms are required for inclusion of GORD patients in clinical trials in which recruitment of patients is based on symptoms only, no matter whether the primary endpoint refers to endoscopy or symptoms only.
rules.exceptions: Slightly different criteria apply to PPI partial responder populations (Section 4.1.2).

rules.rule_text: For trials seeking only a symptomatic treatment claim, endoscopic status must nonetheless be documented; pre-inclusion endoscopy results must not be older than 1 year, or previously diagnosed reflux oesophagitis must be re-checked for healing at time of inclusion.
rules.verbatim_quote: For substances where a 'symptomatic claim' only is being sought, endoscopic status should nevertheless be documented. To this end, either pre-inclusion endoscopy results should be available, which should not be older than 1 year, or – in pre-treated patients (e.g. as 'add-on' to acid suppressive therapy), previously diagnosed reflux oesophagitis should be re-checked for healing at the time of inclusion.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: Patients with 'alarm features' (odynophagia, severe dysphagia, bleeding, weight loss, anaemia, blood in stool) suggesting possible malignant GI disease must be excluded from GORD clinical trials.
rules.verbatim_quote: Patients with so-called 'alarm features' in symptomatology, like odynophagia, severe dysphagia, bleeding, weight loss, anaemia, and blood in stool, pointing to a possible malignant disease of the GI tract should be excluded from clinical trials in GORD. Exclusion can be based on symptoms only.
rules.exceptions: Patients displaying alarm symptoms alongside typical GORD symptoms may be included based on endoscopic exclusion of malignancy.

rules.rule_text: Patients with predominant eosinophilic oesophagitis features (dysphagia and food impaction) must be excluded by endoscopy and biopsy; exclusion based on typical symptoms alone is acceptable if co-existing significant dysphagia and food impaction are absent.
rules.verbatim_quote: The exclusion of patients based on a predominance of the 'typical' eosinophilic oesophagitis symptoms only (as above) is considered acceptable. However, in patients with a predominance of 'typical' symptoms and co-existing significant dysphagia and food impaction, the syndrome should be excluded by endoscopy and biopsy.
rules.exceptions: Exclusion based on symptoms alone acceptable when dysphagia/food impaction are absent.

rules.rule_text: pH monitoring (24-hour or 48-hour ambulatory) is the recommended method for documenting pharmacodynamics of acid suppressive substances and substances influencing LOS/oesophageal pressure in Phase I and II development; pH thresholds (% time pH<4) and observation duration must be defined and justified in advance.
rules.verbatim_quote: Usually 24 hour (or 48 hour) recordings of the pH are used and a maximum time for which pH is allowed to fall below the threshold of 4 is defined as being pathologic. Thresholds (for percentage of time pH<4) and duration of observation should be defined and justified in advance.
rules.exceptions: pH monitoring may be used as inclusion criterion but is not compulsory given diagnostic burden on patients.

rules.rule_text: pH/impedance monitoring is the method of choice in PPI non-responders and partial responders; it is recommended for substances influencing motility and/or oesophageal sphincter pressure to fully document pharmacodynamic properties and dose response in Phase I and II.
rules.verbatim_quote: Impedance monitoring or pH-impedance monitoring is considered to be the method of choice in patients unresponsive or only partly responsive to acid suppressive therapy. The method is recommended for use especially in substances which aim to influence the motility and/or pressure of the oesophagus/oesophageal sphincter in order to fully document the pharmacodynamic properties and dose response in phase I and II of the clinical development.
rules.exceptions: Inclusion of pH/IMP in Phase III trials is not recommended due to impracticability.

rules.rule_text: The Los Angeles classification must be used for patient inclusion/exclusion and as the endoscopic efficacy criterion in clinical trials for erosive GORD.
rules.verbatim_quote: The use of the 'Los Angeles classification' is recommended for inclusion or exclusion of patients and as efficacy criterion in clinical trials for erosive disease (see 4.1.).
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: A fully validated, GORD-specific symptom assessment tool must be used whenever patients are included or evaluated based on symptoms; symptom evaluation must not be validated in the same confirmatory trial where it is used as the primary outcome measure.
rules.verbatim_quote: whenever patients are included or evaluated based on symptoms, a thoroughly and sufficiently validated tool for the assessment of symptoms should be used. [...] It is, however, considered undesirable that validation of diagnostic or efficacy tools and their use as outcome measures takes place in the same trial.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: Symptom assessment must be performed by patients themselves (patient self-report); investigator-rated symptom evaluation is not considered reliable as a primary endpoint, though it may serve as a secondary endpoint.
rules.verbatim_quote: Symptoms should always be assessed by the patients themselves because symptom evaluation by physicians/investigators is considered less reliable. However, symptom assessment done by the investigator may be useful as a secondary endpoint.
rules.exceptions: Investigator assessment acceptable as secondary endpoint.

rules.rule_text: Symptom questionnaires must use VAS or Likert scales with at least 5 rating points and must include both heartburn and acid regurgitation; dichotomous answer modes (e.g. 'adequate relief') are not acceptable as they are not validated in GORD.
rules.verbatim_quote: The symptom questionnaires should use VAS or several point Likert scales for different domains of complaints. The number of rating points (in the Likert scales) within these scales may vary between the tools, however, they should include at least 5 points. The main symptoms of GORD, identified as heartburn and acid regurgitation should be included in the scales. Symptom scales open to deterioration are preferred to dichotomous modes of answers (e.g. like 'satisfactory relief' or 'adequate relief') as the latter have not been validated nor used in GORD.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: The primary efficacy analysis must be a responder analysis based on severity and frequency of both heartburn and acid regurgitation; the protocol must pre-define the definition of a treatment responder and the minimally clinically relevant change in responder rates.
rules.verbatim_quote: The primary analysis of efficacy should be established on a responder analysis based on the evaluation of the two cardinal symptoms of reflux disease, heartburn and acid regurgitation, ideally based on the evaluation of severity and frequency. The protocol should define clearly a treatment responder, i.e. amount of improvement that is considered to be clinically relevant. [...] A minimal clinically relevant change in the overall symptom scale (and its definition) should be included in the validation of such scales and a minimally clinically relevant change in responder rates should be pre-defined.
rules.exceptions: Complete resolution of symptoms is also considered acceptable as primary endpoint.

rules.rule_text: Health-related quality of life (HRQoL) must be included as an important secondary endpoint in GORD trials not explicitly investigating oesophagitis healing; only validated HRQoL questionnaires should be used, with disease-specific instruments preferred over generic ones.
rules.verbatim_quote: Quality of Life has therefore to be regarded as an important secondary endpoint in trials not explicitly investigating the healing of oesophagitis (where the symptom evaluation is the main secondary endpoint). Only validated health-related quality of life questionnaire should be used. [...] To be used as a main secondary endpoint, disease specific questionnaires are preferred to generic instruments.
rules.exceptions: In oesophagitis healing trials, symptom evaluation replaces QoL as the main secondary endpoint.

rules.rule_text: Due to the high prevalence of GORD and likelihood of polypharmacy, a risk-based DDI evaluation using in vitro and animal data and prescription co-medication data must be conducted; the EMA Note for Guidance on Drug Interactions applies.
rules.verbatim_quote: due to the high prevalence of the disease and a high probability that patients will be taking concomitant medications, careful evaluation of potential drug-drug interactions should be conducted. A risk based approach based on in-vitro and animal data and the assessment of prescription data of (co-(prescribed)) drugs is recommended. Regarding drug-drug interactions, the 'Note for Guidance on the investigation of drug interactions' (CPMP/EWP/560/95 and CHMP/EWP/297931/08) should be taken into account.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: Large randomised, double-blind trials are required as the standard design for proof of efficacy in GORD.
rules.verbatim_quote: Large randomised, double-blind treatment trials are required for the proof of efficacy.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: Trials enrolling patients pre-treated with acid suppressive therapy must include an appropriate washout period in the protocol (typically 1 week for H2-antagonists, 4 weeks for PPIs); this does not apply when an add-on indication to acid suppressives is being investigated.
rules.verbatim_quote: Prior to the start of trials that include patients pre-treated with acid-suppressive medication, usually an appropriate wash-out period should be part of the protocol (e.g. one week in case of H2-antagonists, and 4 weeks in the case of PPIs). However, this is not applicable in case an add-on indication to acid suppressives is investigated.
rules.exceptions: Add-on indication trials require a run-in period under active treatment instead.

rules.rule_text: The minimum trial duration for acute treatment of reflux oesophagitis (erosive disease) is 8 weeks; the primary endpoint must be complete healing of all mucosal breaks at end of trial.
rules.verbatim_quote: A trial duration of 8 weeks will be regarded as the minimum requirement for the documentation of healing of reflux oesophagitis. The primary endpoint is the complete healing of all mucosal breaks (see also 6.2.2.) at the end of the trial period.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: Maintenance therapy trials must be at least 6 months in duration to document long-term efficacy; for new chemical entities without prior long-term human safety data, at least 1 year of treatment data is necessary for patient safety documentation.
rules.verbatim_quote: The duration of trials in maintenance therapy should be at least 6 months to sufficiently document long-term efficacy. At least one year treatment data are, however, necessary to appropriately document patient safety for new chemical entities for which no prior human long-term safety data are available.
rules.exceptions: The 1-year safety data requirement applies specifically to NCEs without prior long-term human data.

rules.rule_text: A possible rebound effect after end of treatment must be evaluated during an appropriate follow-up period in all GORD trials.
rules.verbatim_quote: A possible rebound effect after the end of treatment should be evaluated during an appropriate follow-up period.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: For maintenance of effect in erosive disease, the primary endpoint must be the maintenance of complete oesophageal healing over the complete duration of the study; for non-erosive disease maintenance, the maintenance of 'response' (as defined in the acute studies) over the whole trial duration is the proposed primary endpoint.
rules.verbatim_quote: If the maintencance of effect in patients with previous erosive disease is investigated, the endpoint should be the maintenance of complete oesophageal healing over the complete duration of the study. For maintenance treatment in non-erosive disease, the maintenance of 'response' (according to the definition used in the acute studies) over the whole duration of the trial is proposed as the primary endpoint.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: Placebo-controlled trials are ethically not justifiable in typical erosive GORD patients; an appropriate PPI must be used as comparator. Non-inferiority margins versus PPIs must account for superiority of PPIs over placebo AND over other GORD treatments (e.g. H2-antagonists).
rules.verbatim_quote: the use of placebo in the investigation of a typical GORD population appears ethically not justifiable and an appropriate PPI should be used as comparator. For candidate drugs being investigated in comparison to PPIs proposing similar efficacy the non-inferiority margin chosen should not only take into account the magnitude of superiority of the PPIs to placebo, but also to other substances used in the treatment of GORD (e.g. H2-antagonists).
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: Trials in non-erosive GORD should be placebo-controlled; before inclusion, the existence of erosive disease must be excluded by historic/current endoscopy, and the possible development of reflux oesophagitis during active treatment must be investigated and excluded.
rules.verbatim_quote: Trials in 'non-erosive' reflux disease should be conducted in comparison to placebo. [...] before inclusion in such trials, the existence of erosive disease should be excluded, e.g. by historic endoscopy/current endoscopy combined. [...] For such a programme in non-erosive disease, the (possible) development of reflux oesophagitis in relevant numbers of patients while on active treatment should be properly investigated and excluded during phase II.
rules.exceptions: PPI partial responders (add-on therapy) require placebo as mandatory comparator.

rules.rule_text: Paediatric PK studies in each separate age-group are required for GORD drug development; dose-finding must be performed and a supportive rationale provided in children.
rules.verbatim_quote: As pharmacokinetics may be different in children with GORD, separate PK studies in the different age-groups are required. Dose-finding has to be performed and a supportive rationale provided in children as well.
rules.exceptions: Extrapolation of adult efficacy data to adolescent population may be possible when adult efficacy/safety are well-established and specific safety problems are addressed.

rules.rule_text: In infants and younger children, combined pH monitoring and impedance monitoring (pH/IMP) is the recommended method for pharmacodynamic assessment of acid inhibitors; combined pH/IMP monitoring and manometry are required for pro-motility drugs in PK/PD studies.
rules.verbatim_quote: in infants and younger children, combined pH monitoring and impedance monitoring (pH/IMP) are recommended. [...] For other treatment modalities that target the frequency of GOR episodes impedance monitoring and manometry should be used in PK/PD studies for the assessment of a pharmacodynamic effect on the frequency of bolus reflux and transient lower oesophageal sphincter relaxation episodes.
rules.exceptions: If pH/IMP monitoring-symptom association has been sufficiently explored in Phase II, pH/IMP co-primary endpoint in Phase III may be spared.

rules.rule_text: Confirmatory paediatric GORD studies must be double-blind, randomised controlled trials (RCT) that include clinical efficacy and safety data in addition to PK/PD data.
rules.verbatim_quote: For the indication of paediatric GORD, clinical efficacy and safety data are needed in addition to PK/PD data. Confirmatory studies should be double-blind, randomised controlled trials (RCT).
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: For paediatric erosive GORD trials, the primary endpoint must be complete healing of the oesophageal mucosa confirmed by endoscopy; recommended trial duration is 8-12 weeks with 2-4 weeks of follow-up; biopsies must be taken during any endoscopy until the value of histology is resolved.
rules.verbatim_quote: The primary endpoint should be complete healing of the oesophageal mucosa. Endoscopy is needed to confirm the presence and severity of erosive oesophagitis and to exclude other diseases. [...] Recommended duration of trials is 8-12 weeks with 2-4 weeks of follow-up. [...] biopsies should be taken during any endoscopy as long as this issue is not resolved.
rules.exceptions: Relapses are uncommon after successful healing in children; maintenance studies generally not required except for secondary GORD with oesophagitis.

rules.rule_text: For paediatric symptomatic GORD trials (12-17 years), erosive and eosinophilic oesophagitis must be excluded by previous or baseline endoscopy, and a baseline Helicobacter pylori test must be performed; children with Hp-associated gastroduodenal disease must not be included.
rules.verbatim_quote: In trials for the symptomatic treatment of GORD in children, erosive oesophagitis and eosinophilic oesophagitis should be excluded by previous or baseline endoscopy, a test for Helicobacter pylori (Hp) should be performed at baseline and children with Hp associated gastroduodenal disease should not be included in the trial.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: For younger children (2-11 years) with symptomatic GORD, patient inclusion must be based on combined pH/IMP monitoring-symptom association results; enrolment based only on clinical symptoms and endoscopic findings without confirmed symptom-reflux link is unacceptable when a symptom benefit claim is made.
rules.verbatim_quote: Enrolment based on clinical symptoms and endoscopic findings without confirmation of a link between symptoms and reflux is unacceptable if symptom benefit is claimed, therefore, inclusion of patients should be based on the combined pH/IMP monitoring - symptom association results.
rules.exceptions: Absence of endoscopy may be acceptable with justification given the very low prevalence of oesophagitis in this age group.

rules.rule_text: For infant GORD trials (0-<2 years), diagnosis must use validated symptom-based questionnaires (e.g. I-GERQ/I-GERQ-R) combined with pH/IMP monitoring; only infants failing conservative management (feeding and positioning) may be included; eosinophilic oesophagitis and food allergy must be excluded.
rules.verbatim_quote: Diagnosis of GORD should be made using validated symptom-based questionnaires, such as the I-GERQ with the addition - especially important in this low age group - of combined pH/IMP monitoring with symptom association. [...] Only those children in whom changes in feeding and positioning have not resulted in a satisfactory reduction of symptoms should be included in trials of new drugs for GORD. Eosinophilic oesophagitis and food allergy (e.g. cow milk) should be excluded clinically and/or endoscopically.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: GORD drug development must comply with ICH E1 requirements for long-term safety documentation because GORD is a chronic disease; safety data collected in sub-populations do not automatically support authorisation in a wider patient population.
rules.verbatim_quote: The requirements of the ICH E1 guideline for symptomatic benign disorder will be applicable, as GORD is regarded as a chronic disease and this makes it necessary to appropriately document the long-term safety of such compounds (see also. 6.3.1.). [...] Safety data collected in sub-populations may not necessarily support the authorisation in a wider patient population.
rules.exceptions: None stated.
